Behavioral Rule: Slowloris Partial HTTP Connection DoS

MITRE ATT&CK: Impact | T1499 — Endpoint Denial of Service (T1499.001 - OS Exhaustion Flood)

Classtype: denial-of-service
Severity: Medium
Context: Slowloris holds HTTP connections open by sending partial HTTP request headers very slowly, never completing the request. Classtype: denial-of-service
Severity: High

HTTP floods (BHR-005, BHR-008) complete the handshake and send actual HTTP data. Port scan (BHR-015) also uses SYN-only but at a lower rate and targets MULTIPLE ports sequentially, while SYN flood targets the SAME port at very high rate.

Severity: Low

BHR-021 is the most generic probe indicator — it catches probes that don't fit the specific patterns of other rules.

Behavioral Rule: Internal Network Infiltration — Lateral Movement
Protocol: TCP | Ports: dst_port:445,3389,135,139,22,80,443
Keywords: infiltration, lateral movement, internal scan, SMB, RDP, WMI, pivot, post-exploitation, dropper, internal reconnaissance, Infilteration
MITRE ATT&CK: Lateral Movement | T1021 — Remote Services (T1021.001 - Remote Desktop Protocol / T1021.002 - SMB/Windows Admin Shares / T1021.004 - SSH)
Detection: Connections to internal administrative ports: SMB 445, RDP 3389, WMI 135; Short probe flows followed by longer established sessions; Authentication exchange traffic: balanced byte ratio; Source is an internal host connecting to other internal hosts on admin ports; Sequential connection attempts to multiple internal hosts: lateral sweep; Mix of failed (short RST) and successful (longer established) connections; Internal-to-internal traffic on ports not typically used between those hosts
Flow profile: direction=internal_to_internal; duration=mixed (short probes + longer sessions on success); payload=varies (probe=minimal, session=moderate); handshake=complete on successful connections, RST on failed; byte_ratio=balanced (authentication exchanges are bidirectional); packet_pattern=sequential_targets, mixed_durations; tcp_flags=SYN-ACK-FIN normal for success, SYN-RST for failure
Classtype: attempted-admin
Severity: High
Context: Infiltration involves an attacker who has gained an initial foothold (via dropper, phishing, or exploit) and then moves laterally through the internal network to access additional systems. Flow-level indicators include an internal host connecting to administrative ports (SMB 445, RDP 3389, WMI 135, SSH 22) on other internal hosts, especially when that source host does not normally access those services. The attack pattern shows sequential connection attempts across multiple targets — short failed connections (RST) interspersed with longer successful sessions. The CIC-IDS2018 Infiltration scenario involves compromising an internal host via a dropper and then using it to scan and attack other internal targets.
Differential diagnosis: Infiltration (BHR-015) is INTERNAL-TO-INTERNAL on ADMINISTRATIVE ports with mixed probe/session patterns. Port scans (BHR-016, BHR-017) are typically external-to-internal or scan MANY ports on a single host. SSH brute force (BHR-010) targets a SINGLE host on port 22 repeatedly, while infiltration connects to MULTIPLE different internal hosts across various admin ports.

Behavioral Rule: RST Termination Without FIN — Abnormal TCP Teardown
Protocol: TCP
Keywords: RST, abnormal teardown, no FIN, connection reset, malware teardown, C2 disconnect, exploit disconnect
MITRE ATT&CK: Command and Control | T1095 — Non-Application Layer Protocol
Detection: RST flag present with no FIN flag; Connection abruptly reset without graceful close; Multiple packets before RST: established flow then reset; Seen in port scanning, C2 beaconing, and exploit attempts
Flow profile: handshake=may_be_complete; packet_pattern=normal_flow_then_abrupt_RST; tcp_flags=RST without FIN
Classtype: misc-activity
Severity: Low
Context: RST termination without a preceding FIN indicates an abnormal connection close. Legitimate connections typically use FIN for graceful teardown. RST-only teardown is common in: port scanners that reset after banner grab, C2 malware that abruptly closes connections after data exchange, and exploit frameworks. On its own this is low severity, but combined with other indicators (non-standard window, proxy port, short duration) it contributes to a botnet or infiltration pattern. This rule acts as a supporting signal for other rules.
Differential diagnosis: RST without FIN (BHR-020) is a general-purpose supporting indicator that can appear in many attack types. BHR-002 specifically combines PSH+RST in a micro-burst pattern on C2 ports. BHR-020 is broader and lower severity — it flags the abnormal teardown behavior without attributing it to a specific attack type.
